Please describe your research career to date
I am a dietitian and developing clinical-academic who strives to improve the nutritional care of patients with head
and neck cancer (HNC) through applying evidence-based nutritional interventions into practice. Witnessing the
positive impact nutrition has on physical/psychosocial wellbeing motivates me to pursue an independent role in
research and contribute new knowledge to improve/innovate nutritional care.
Research experience
My enthusiasm for research started during my undergraduate dissertation which investigated the diets of university
athletes. I gained ethical approval, recruited six participants, collected dietary data, and explored dietary choices
through a focus group. I developed skills in statistical (using SPSS) and

agement skills through seconding as Clinical-Lead HNC Dietitian twice (over
2-years). My responsibilities included team management, complex workforce planning amid COVID-19, staff
recruitment/retention, developing business cases and utilising research funding within a clinical service. I have
contributed to coroners’ inquests, instilling in me the importance of evidence when justifying clinical decisions.
I am applying for this fellowship as though I have made great progress as a developing clinical-academic, this is
the crucial next step in becoming a clinical research leader. My ambitions include leading a research programme
in perioperative nutrition at UCL, supporting UCLH become an ERAS Centre of Excellence and nurturing dietetic
talent to produce a sustainable team of clinical academics.

Proposed training and development programme
My existing research experience helped me develop skills in quantitative research, trial delivery, dissemination,
and academic writing for publication. The Vitae-RDF helped me identify gaps in my learning to deliver the
proposed studies alongside clinical practice training and placements. My training plan supports my long-term
aspirations of becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group in perioperative
nutrition at UCL and improving ERAS at UCLH. The GANTT chart included indicates key milestones in my training
programme.
Proposed formal study and academic training
As a PhD student at UCL, I will access the comprehensive doctoral skills development programme which includes
PhD training modules such as preparing a thesis. I will also attend courses on Data Management, Zotero, Creating
Infographics and Designing an Impactful Poster to equip me with secure data collection, referencing management,
and dissemination. Whilst I have undergone several PPI workshops, I found facilitating my group challenging and
would benefit from further training through undertaking the Facilitation of PPI activities workshop.

Clinical academic development
Extend skills in Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS)
My research and clinical interests align well with the topic of ERAS. Current ERAS Centres of Excellence are
mainly led by colorectal surgeons. A gap exists for an HNC-led Centre of Excellence – it is my aspiration to play a
leading role in such a centre at UCLH.
To achieve this, I will attend the ERAS Society Course to develop my knowledge of key ERAS principles and visit
two leading/founding ERAS Centres of Excellence in Stockholm. This includes the Karolinska Institute and Ersta-
Diakoni Hospital, under the supervision of Anna Garpenbeck (ERAS coordinating nurse at Ersta-Diakoni and a
board member of the Swedish ERAS Society). I will also visit Professor Bill Fawcett and Dr Chris Jones
(Consultant Anaesthetists and ERAS Society committee members) at the Royal Surrey Hospital to learn how to
set up an ERAS Centre of Excellence in the UK/NHS.

evidence.text:
ate-PIs, at the BAHNO conference. I was invited to share my experience at the NIHR conference:
Research, the NHS and you; improving awareness of clinical research opportunities accessible in practice.
Furthermore, when a studentship for MSc Clinical Nutrition tuition-fees was advertised, I successfully applied and
completed alongside full-time clinical work which included taught modules in ‘Research Methods’. I developed
academic writing skills through my dissertation, which was a quasi-experimental study assessing participant
adherence with intermittent-fasting diets, published in Clinical Nutrition-ESPEN. I was awarded the UCL Dean’s
List Commendation in recognition of outstanding academic achievement.
Collaboration and professional engagement
I have developed national and international networks though election to the BAHNO-Council (representing
dietetics) and BAHNO-International Collaboration Committee. I have helped organise four annual BAHNO
conferences to-date by marking abstracts and chairing sessions.
I co-authored national standards published in Journal of Oral Pathology; the benchmark document for quality
assurance in HNC used nationally and abroad. I led ENT-UK national

guidance for dietetics; the key document
that guides practitioners on optimal service provision/delivery, used in NHS business cases to fund dietetic
services (accepted in Journal of Laryngology; first-author). I worked with Restorative Dentistry UK, contributing a
dietetics article for the British Dental Journal HNC-edition. This provided guidance on mitigating risks of oral
nutritional supplements on oral health and made the 2022 British Dental Journal ‘Big Impact Collection’.
I am gaining recognition in cancer nutrition, receiving invitations to:
 Peer-review manuscripts for journals including Supportive Care Cancer and university healthcare ethics
applications, developing my critical-appraisal skills.
 Speak at London Sarcoma and UCLH-Support in Cancer Research Forum, developing my presentation
skills.
 Review resources for Sarcoma-UK and World Cancer Research Fund, developing my public-engagement
skills.
 Join the British Dietetic Association (BDA)-HNC Committee.
 Lecture on UCL nutrition/dietetics degrees.
Supervisory and teaching
I completed a 10-month secondment as a Teaching Fellow at Kings College London, developing my educational
and leadership skills. I co-led, red

esigned, and revalidated a 30-credit dietetics module. I was personal tutor to five
students, providing pastoral care.
I represent dietetics on HEE Aspirant Cancer-Career and Education-Development programme (workstream-1),
reviewing e-learning modules in cancer-care, which increased knowledge and understanding amongst aspirant
cancer professionals (published in British Journal of Nursing; co-author).
I have successfully supervised 6 MSc and 2 BSc dissertations to completion. One student audited weight change
during radiotherapy. This raised awareness of weight loss risks amongst patients with obesity. We published the
abstract in Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics and were awarded the ‘Best Abstract Prize’ at the BDA
Research Symposium.
Professional experience
I have developed specialist skills in HNC through advanced communication training and MSc Cancer-Care
modules (Royal Marsden School). I have extensive clinical supervision experience, having developed staff-training
competencies in HNC. I was the department’s student-training co-lead 2019-2020 and helped set-up the
departments research group to develop a research culture in dietetics.
I have developed leadership and man

agement skills through seconding as Clinical-Lead HNC Dietitian twice (over
2-years). My responsibilities included team management, complex workforce planning amid COVID-19, staff

evidence.text:
Aim:
This study aims to improve the nutrition of patients with head and neck cancer when moving from tube-feeding to
eating by mouth, after surgery.
Why is this research important?
In the UK, 12,400 people are diagnosed with head and neck cancer each year, of which 75% have surgery. This
may involve removing parts of the mouth used for eating/drinking, such as the tongue. The surgeons will rebuild
these by using tissue from another part of the body, such as the arm. This is called a ‘flap’.
After surgery, patients do not eat or drink for six to twelve days, and nutrition is provided by feeding tube either
through the nose or directly into the stomach. The type of tube placed, the timeframe for re-starting eating/drinking
and when the tube can be removed depends on many factors. These include being able to confidently swallow
enough food/drink safely (affected by the amount of swelling in the mouth) and local hospital policies for when
tubes are removed. Generally, the surgeon and team decide this for each person. However, nose tubes are often
removed as soon as eating/drinking has begun, and before patients can manage enough food/drink. This happens
because these tubes can prevent
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
going home in some hospitals. Previous research shows 40-72% patients do not
eat/drink enough after surgery. Nutrition drastically affects recovery and is the third largest cost-save to the NHS.
Good nutrition speeds up recovery, improves wound healing, survival, and wellbeing. Poor nutrition slows
recovery, increases risks of infection and even death.
How will I do this?
We want to understand what factors help or stop patients restart eating/drinking enough so that feeding tubes can
be removed at the right time. Also, we think some patients can eat/drink sooner. This will improve their nutrition
and help swallowing muscles recover.
 We will first review research articles that have studied patients moving from tube-feeding to
eating/drinking.
 We will then survey healthcare staff including surgeons, nurses, dietitians, and speech therapists across
sixty-five UK hospitals. We will find out how practices differ including when patients restart eating/drinking
and what types of tubes patients go home with.
 We will use the information from the survey and articles to decide what data we will collect from hospital
records. We will also decide which staff practices we will watch and qu
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
estions we need to ask patients and
staff.
When it comes to eating/drinking, the hospital data will tell us when patients restart, whether they have enough,
and reasons for delayed or not enough eating/drinking. The questions and practices watched will help us
understand how patients find eating/drinking after surgery and how staff help them.
Findings will be used to develop a staff toolkit to improve feeding practices and nutrition after surgery.
How have patients been involved?
We discussed this project with patients who have had flap surgery. They told us that this research felt meaningful
to them, and they lacked confidence with restarting eating/drinking as this often occurred abruptly and with little
support. This made them feel isolated. We will work with patients across all stages of this project to:
 Alert us to missed areas.
 Check study information sheets are clear.
 Check the data we plan to collect.
 Check questions we will ask patients and staff to ensure these are clear/relevant.
 Review patient interview logs to check we understand the patients’ viewpoint.
How will findings be shared?
We will share with patients through social media, blogs, our funder's website

, charities and support-groups by
making an animated video of findings. We will share with staff working in head and neck cancer by writing articles
and speaking at meetings.

This research should prevent both developing and/or exacerbating malnutrition through optimising post-operative
nutrition, and decrease readmissions associated with nutrition/hydration but also wound healing, as this is directly
associated with poor nutrition.
Review of existing evidence
Early oral feeding is well established in treatment of other cancer types28 due to benefits including reduced LoS
and improved post-operative nutritional adequacy with calls for high-quality research in HNC29. Five retrospective
studies5,7,9,30,31and one pilot randomised controlled trial (RCT)32have been conducted in HNC flap-surgery with
another RCT registered (NCT04787939). These are limited by inconsistent definitions of early oral feeding (which
a registered scoping review aims to determine33) and selection bias6. Firstly, only patients without a tracheostomy
were randomised to early oral feeding. Tracheostomy-use varies between centres. In centres that treat early-stage
disease, tracheostomy-use is 13%9. In centres that treat more advanced-stage disease, tracheostomy-use is
88%31.Advanced-stage disease makes up ~59% of cases34. Secondly, patients with previous radiotherapy (~30%

of cases) were excluded30,35.
Current trials exclude most patients having flap-surgery meaning findings have limited uptake. To avoid excluding
patients, safety evidence is required on perceived risk factors for oral feeding commencement. Two retrospective
studies have started to establish these31,36.However, naso-gastric tube presence on discharge was used as a
binary outcome measure for oral feeding resumption. These are not mutually exclusive as tube and oral feeding
can occur concurrently. Also, not all hospitals permit discharge with nasogastric tubes which the proposed survey
will map. Neither study recorded availability of suitable hospital meals or patient-centred factors, such as
confidence with eating/drinking. Type of diets initiated were described as ‘fluids, puree and normal’ which are not
validated descriptors. Staff attitudes/beliefs towards early oral feeding have yet been explored and are a potential
barrier to change9, given surgeons report favouring their trainer’s practice over RCTs in other cancer types
advocating early oral feeding37–39.
Consensus guidelines for nutrition in surgery recommend continuing tube-feeding in patients unable to meet their
requiremen

ts orally27. Recommendations are based on RCTs in gastrointestinal and orthopaedic surgery which
found that continued tube-feeding prevents weight loss, reduces malnutrition and improves treatment tolerance
and quality-of-life compared to oral intake only40. In HNC, one retrospective study8 has assessed whether patients
meet their nutritional requirements for 10-days post-surgery. 40% had inadequate intake, defined as meeting 60%
robust27
of estimated nutritional requirements. A 75% cut-off is considered more and when applied, has found 75%
do not meet requirements41. No data was collected on protein intake. The early feeding RCT32 also measured
nutritional adequacy for 3-days post-operatively. Findings lack accuracy as adequacy was based on nutritional
requirements calculated from average weights of 50-70-year-olds in China, rather than actual participant weights.
Furthermore, the trial was run in 2022 but used weight data from 2014, which won’t capture demographic changes,
as more patients with HNC now present overweight/obese42;who are more likely to have inadequate intakes post-
operatively8.
Neither study provided information on whether patients with inadequate intakes continu

ed tube-
feeding or follow-up information on how this may have impacted on nutritional status.
Existing studies are limited by selection bias, follow-up period, non-validated measures, and have missed factors
reported by patients to be important. These gaps will be remedied by the proposed prospective cohort study. This
will run in a centralised HNC centre which will include patients with advanced-stage disease, previous
radiotherapy, and tracheostomy. Nutritional status will be measured at baseline and 30-days post-surgery using
the PG-SGA (patient-generated subjective global assessment), which is considered gold-standard in HNC. A 30-
day follow-up can capture clinically significant changes in nutritional status. Factors previously missed will be
recorded including pre/post-operative swallowing function, hospital meal availability, and type of diet (using
validated IDDSI-International Dysphagia Diet Standardisation Initiative descriptiors43). Nutritional requirements will
be estimated using best-practice equations calculated from actual weights. Energy and protein intake will be
measured using the 75% cut-off for adequacy and information recorded on whether patients with inadequa

te
intakes continued with tube-feeding. The proposed qualitative studies will capture patient-centred factors and staff
attitudes/behaviours by drawing on theories that identify influences on health professional behaviour to address
barriers for implementing change.
What is the research question/aims and objectives?
Research question:
What are the post-operative feeding practices and nutritional intake of patients with HNC transitioning from tube to
oral intake after undergoing flap-surgery, and how can practices and intake be optimised?
Aim:
To investigate post-operative feeding practices and nutritional intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery and to develop a toolkit for timely and adequate intake. The nature of
the toolkit will be determined by conducting exploratory work (WS1-4) as part of evidence-synthesis.
Objectives:
1. To examine post-operative feeding practices and nutritional intake when transitioning patients from tube to
oral feeding following flap-surgery (WS1).
2. To describe national post-operative feeding practices on when patients commence oral feeding and how
they are transitioned off tube-feeding followin

Underpinning theory
This study is based on the Medical Research Council (MRC) guidance on complex interventions, specifically
focussing on phase 1, intervention development44. This will draw on normalisation process theory (NPT)45to
understand the organisational structures that influence implementation of complex interventions and the theoretical
(TDF)46to

Rationale:

mplementable in different units,

WS3: Prospective cohort study [Months 10-29]
Rationale:
Limited empirical research has identified factors associated with timely/adequate nutritional intake after surgery. A
prospective cohort study will identify factors that may be predictive of nutritional adequacy that can be targeted in
the toolkit.
Research question:
What are the factors that contribute to timely and adequate oral intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
An observational single-centre exploratory prospective cohort study will be undertaken at UCLH which is a
centralised London service for four NHS-trusts, covering a wide-geographic area representative of people from
diverse, multiple ethnic backgrounds affected by HNC3,52,53.UCLH offers all discharge pathways for oral/tube
feeding which optimises generalisability, within the limitations of a single-centre design.
Data will be collected from electronic hospital records. All measures are routinely gathered except for the following
patient-recorded measures:
1-week diet diary once oral feeding commences.

 Surgical characteristics: reconstructive site/flap, defect-volume (cm3), neck-dissection, tracheostomy
presence/weaning.
 Postoperative LoS.
 Complications (Clavien-Dindo classification) and re-admissions within 30-days of surgery.

Cost-saving is anticipated by avoiding malnutrition and 9% (unplanned) and 72% (planned) readmissions caused
by nutrition. Readmission costs from flap-surgery have been evaluated in the USA ($9,070-$15,916/patient). Most
comparable UK estimates comes from ear/nose/throat surgery (£1052/patient)72. However, average readmission

WS1: Scoping review [Months 1-7]
Rationale:
There are currently no reviews published or registere

d investigating post-operative feeding practices and
nutritional intake after HNC flap-surgery. A systematic review registered (CRD42022380436) aims to assess
whether early oral feeding influences wound healing, swallowing-function, and LoS. Nutritional outcomes are not
being assessed. A scoping review will map practices and nutritional intake, identify knowledge-gaps and
compliment findings from the registered reviews.
Methods:
Scoping review ineligible for PROSPERO and therefore it will be registered with Open Science Framework or
Figshare.
Arksey and O’Malley (2005) 6-step framework47:
1. Research question: What are the post-operative feeding practices and nutritional intake of patients with
HNC transitioning from tube to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
2. Data extraction: Systematic literature search conducted with no date limits.
3. Study selection: Initial screening of all titles/abstracts and then full articles by myself and a second
investigator independently against criteria:
Inclusion criteria:
 Adults>18 years
 HNC flap-surgery
Focus on practices, timing/adequacy when transitioning from tube to oral feeding.

 English language
 Original research, case-re

ports, conference-abstracts, guidelines and grey-literature
Exclusion criteria:
Animal studies

 Children/adolescents
 Non-english
Cohen’s-K statistic will be calculated to determine inter-rater agreement with discordant results reviewed
by a third investigator.
4. Charting: Study characteristics will be extracted and compiled for coding/validation using Covidence
software.
5. Reporting: Results will be reported using PRISMA-Scr and mapped according to sources/types of
evidence and limitations reported e.g., any relevant sources omitted.
6. Consultation/dissemination: Findings will be discussed with PPI to capture insights not identified. Findings
will inform cohort study measures, survey and interview questions.
WS2: National Survey [Months 3-7]
Rationale:
Variation exists for timing to oral intake and processes for transitioning off tube-feeding5,9,14,31. This is associated
with local resources for placing gastrostomies and policies for discharging with naso-gastric tubes. A survey will
describe current reality and canvass opinions to understand the contexts the toolkit will be implemented.
Design:
A cross-sectional open web-based survey circulated within the UK.
Survey questi

ons will be developed using the review findings, alongside discussions between the research team
which includes key stakeholders (dietitian, speech-therapist, surgeon) and patients. Content validity will be
method48,by
established using Lawshe’s asking independent healthcare professionals to rate how essential each
question is to determine which to include/exclude.
An online, self-completed semi-structured questionnaire will be developed using Qualtrics software and piloted
with healthcare professionals reflective of the sample frame to ensure functionality. This will include multiple-option
answer, five-fold Likert scales and free-text comments asking about the centre demographic, timing to oral
feeding, tubes placed and removal processes.
Responses will be mandatory and options of ‘not applicable’ or ‘rather not say’ provided to ensure data-
completeness, with an opportunity to review/change answers before submitting.
Sample:
Multi-disciplinary leads at UK/NHS HNC surgical units (n=65) will be identified from the National HNC Audit
list49and approached via email with an explanation of the study and asked to disseminate within their units to staff

Improved nutritional outcomes by identifying facilitators and barriers for achieving nutritional adequacy and optimal

Potential to change perioperative HNC feeding management by developing a toolkit that optimises decision-
making when transitioning patients from tube to oral intake, accounting for variation in local resources and
infrastructure.
NHS/service:
It is anticipated that streamlining oral feeding commencement and avoiding post-operative nutritional deficits will
reduce hospital stays, readmissions and associated costs.

delayed and inadequate intake when transitioning from tube to oral feeding. This aligns with the 2019 James-Lind
Alliance Nutritional Screening and Malnutrition research priority ‘can nutritional-interventions lead to health

Why is this research important?
This research falls under enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) initiatives. It should optimise post-operative

Throughout my career, I have developed research skills in quantitative data analysis, dissemination (journal
publications and conference presentations), and stakeholder engagement through conducting 10 service
evaluations and audits to resolve problems encountered in day-to-day practice. The satisfaction gained from
seeing direct improvements in care makes me appreciate pursuing research within a clinical role. For example,
following concerns that the local protocol for gastrostomy insertion caused unnecessary placement delays, I
audited complications, benchmarked pathways, and formed a stakeholder group to agree policy changes. A re-
audit revealed reduced delays without increased complications, published in Clinical Nutrition-OpenScience.

prolonged periods before recommencing oral intake post-surgery. I published findings in Oral Oncology Reports
and presented at the British Association of Head and Neck Oncologists (BAHNO) conference. I realised that
evidence-gaps were best answered through research and could change perioperative nutritional care. T

WS3: Prospective cohort study [Months 10-29]
Rationale:
Limited empirical research has identified factors associated with timely/adequate nutritional intake after surgery. A
prospective cohort study will identify factors that may be predictive of nutritional adequacy that can be targeted in
the toolkit.
Research question:
What are the factors that contribute to timely and adequate oral intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
An observational single-centre exploratory prospective cohort study will be undertaken at UCLH which is a
centralised London service for four NHS-trusts, covering a wide-geographic area representative of people from
diverse, multiple ethnic backgrounds affected by HNC3,52,53.UCLH offers all discharge pathways for oral/tube
feeding which optimises generalisability, within the limitations of a single-centre design.
Data will be collected from electronic hospital records. All measures are routinely gathered except for the following
patient-recorded measures:
1-week diet diary once oral feeding commences.



estion:
What are the facilitators and barriers for transitioning from tube to adequate oral intake in patients with HNC after
undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
Rapid qualitative research techniques will be utilised to optimise efficiency by running data collection and analysis
in parallel. 1:1 semi-structured interviews will be conducted with patients and healthcare professionals and
unstructured observations of staff practices.
Sample:
Patient interviews:
A subset of patients participating in the cohort study will be invited off-treatment within 6-weeks of surgery.
Patients who have not recommenced oral intake in this timeframe will be excluded. Purposive sampling will be
used to ensure maximal variation in terms of sex, age, ethnicity, disease site/stage, type of flap and feeding tube.
General guidance indicates that 6-12 interviews is usually acceptable with little new information beyond 1560.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a similar study in colorectal cancer, a sample of 10
is anticipated61.
Staff interviews:
Healthcare professionals will be invited to participate using purposive sampling to represent different professional
roles and years

of experience. Sampling will be supported by survey data to capture variation in practices.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a study of similar design, a sample of 16-18
healthcare professionals is anticipated38.
Inclusion:
 >18 years
 Involved in transitioning patients from tube to oral intake after flap-surgery in a UK/NHS HNC post-
operative setting (e.g. surgical-ward, critical-care)
Exclusion:
 Not involved in facilitating re-commencement of oral diet/fluids after flap-surgery in a UK/NHS HNC setting
 Short-term staff e.g., agency
Observations:
Unstructured non-participant observation of surgical ward rounds, multi-disciplinary inpatient, and outpatient
reviews at UCLH. UCLH is a centralised HNC centre for four hospitals and represents all post-operative feeding
practices.
An initial purposive sample (inclusion/exclusion criteria per interviews) of three patient cases (naso-gastric tube,
pre- and post-operative gastrostomy) to capture different practices for transitioning patients from tube to oral
feeding. Observation will be until data saturation is achieved.
Data collection:
Interviews:

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
My existing research experience helped me develop skills in quantitative research, trial delivery, dissemination,
and academic writing for publication. The Vitae-RDF helped me identify gaps in my learning to deliver the
proposed studies alongside clinical practice training and placements. My training plan supports my long-term
aspirations of becoming a Consultant Clinical Academic Dietitian leading a research group in perioperative
nutrition at UCL and improving ERAS at UCLH. The GANTT chart included indicates key milestones in my training
programme.
Proposed formal study and academic training
As a PhD student at UCL, I will access the comprehensive doctoral skills development programme which includes
PhD training modules such as preparing a thesis. I will also attend courses on Data Management, Zotero, Creating
Infographics and Designing an Impactful Poster to equip me with secure data collection, referencing management,
and dissemination. Whilst I have undergone several PPI workshops, I found facilitating my group challenging and
would benefit from further training through undertaking the Facilitation of PPI activities workshop.
To deve
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
lop skills in scoping review methodology, I will attend the online JBI Scoping review workshop at University
of Nottingham and access 1:1 support from a research librarian at UCL to develop my search strategy. I will also
undertake two modules at UCL in Survey Design and Quantitative Survey Research to equip me with design,
conduct and analysis of survey data.
I have previously undertaken quantitative research and completed the UCL Practical Medical Statistics Course
during my pre-doctoral fellowship which has provided a good baseline training in statistics and software (SPSS
and Stata). I will undertake the following courses at UCL to further develop these skills for analysing my cohort
study data: Statistics for observational studies, Regression Analysis and Introduction to R. I have prior experience
using Diet-Plan software for analysing diet diaries, but plan to use Nutritics as this is more robust. Professor
Nathan Davies (Dietetics programme lead at UCL) has agreed to provide me with a license free-of-charge and
attend his practical classes using this software.
Qualitative research methods comprise a key component of this project and I will invest time in developing and
expan
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ding my knowledge and skills in this area. To develop baseline knowledge, I will attend the Qualitative
Research Methods in Health short-course at UCL which includes practical skills for conducting interviews. I will
also undertake the Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Bootcamp at UCL to equip me with using the rapid
methods I am proposing. Having not conducted any previous participant observations, I will attend the
Ethnography for Healthcare Improvement Summer School at University of Leicester. These courses do not include
training on software for coding/analysis, therefore I will also undertake the Introduction to NVivo short-course at
University of Surrey. Lastly, whilst I have published quantitative research, I have not published qualitative research
therefore I will attend the Writing/Publishing Qualitative Research course at UCL.
Finally, central to my research is the development of a toolkit anticipated to be a complex intervention drawing on
implementation science theories. I will therefore attend the Mixed-Methods approaches course at UCL, Developing
and Evaluating Complex Interventions, online-course at University of Glasgow and the Implementation Science
Mastercla
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ss, at KCL to develop my knowledge of implementation planning, development, and evaluation. I will also
undertake the Influencing and Negotiating course at UCL to effectively manage my stakeholder consultation group.
Clinical academic development
Extend skills in Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS)
My research and clinical interests align well with the topic of ERAS. Current ERAS Centres of Excellence are
mainly led by colorectal surgeons. A gap exists for an HNC-led Centre of Excellence – it is my aspiration to play a
leading role in such a centre at UCLH.
To achieve this, I will attend the ERAS Society Course to develop my knowledge of key ERAS principles and visit
two leading/founding ERAS Centres of Excellence in Stockholm. This includes the Karolinska Institute and Ersta-
Diakoni Hospital, under the supervision of Anna Garpenbeck (ERAS coordinating nurse at Ersta-Diakoni and a
board member of the Swedish ERAS Society). I will also visit Professor Bill Fawcett and Dr Chris Jones
(Consultant Anaesthetists and ERAS Society committee members) at the Royal Surrey Hospital to learn how to
set up an ERAS Centre of Excellence in the UK/NHS.
To develop my wider networks and knowledg
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e of perioperative care, I will attend weekly UCL Centre of
Perioperative Medicine multi-disciplinary research/education collaborative meetings. I will shadow flap-surgery and
pre-operative anaesthetic assessments to develop my anatomy/physiology knowledge. Finally, I will shadow
UCLH Prehabilitation team pre-operative dietetic clinics which are underpinned by ERAS principles.
Developing as a clinical research leader
To support development as both a clinical and research leader, I will enrol on the Rosalind Franklin Programme
NHS Leadership Academy online-course. This aligns with my current leadership experience level and is
achievable alongside my PhD. I will also attend the Women in Research Masterclass at UCL to help me promote
gender parity and champion female representation in HNC research, as women are currently underrepresented in
this field74.
To grow the research agenda for dietitians I will attend monthly UCLH Dietetics Research Group meetings and
support Dietetic MSc students and staff with research, audit and service evaluation. I also have 1:1 time agreed
with Dr Rebecca Carpenter (Clinical Research Manager) at Nutricia Medical, Danone to learn about how industry
works

professions, irrespective of grade. This is reflected in the UCLH Strategy for 2023-27, in which we commit to
fostering clinical academic careers within nursing, midwifery and allied health professions (NMAHP). We aim to
achieve the national target of 1% of NMAHP in clinical academic roles by 2030 through identifying NMAHPs who
have the potential to thrive in this role and by supporting them with their career progression. Florence is one of our
staff we are investing in, to become a leader within head and neck dietetics. Florence is currently completing a
Chief Nurse Fellowship programme, similar to the NIHR PCAF, in time and on budget according to her planned
programme of work.
UCLH is one of the founding members of the biomedical research centres and continues to lead on ground-
breaking advances in research for the benefits of patients and sharing best practise with its academic partner
UCL. AHP clinical academics are essential for BRC funded institutions. Florence’s proposed fellowship will be
based across UCL and UCLH, aligning with UCLH Strategy for 2023-27 to deepen collaboration between
UCLH/UCL and broaden the workforce and career opportunities for NMAHPs to improve the di
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e (DSIS), in the Faculty of Medical Sciences at UCL. Our department provides an
excellent environment for early and mid-career researchers, particularly clinical academics. UCL, and specifically
DSIS, is committed to promoting equality, diversity and inclusion, and holds a silver Athena Swan award. Within
DSIS, we currently have over 100 doctoral research students, and RDTI hosts the largest proportion of these
across our three campuses. RDTI is host to seven thriving research centres, each including clinician and career
scientists, covering a range of research portfolios from discovery science through to policy and implementation
research. Our team includes several clinician scientists from allied health professions who will be able to provide
mentorship to Florence as she progresses her studies. RDTI currently hosts several awards from NIHR and UKRI
including an NIHR Research Professor, an MRC Senior Fellow and, in conjunction with UCLH, the NIHR Central
London Patient Safety Research Collaboration (CL-PSRC). Furthermore, the RDTI leadership team include two
theme leads at the UCLH NIHR Biomedical Research Centre, demonstrating our strong working relationship with
our main NHS pa
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
rtner.
Florence’s proposal is well aligned with the strategic direction of the division. She will specifically be based within
the UCL Head and Neck Academic Centre (HNAC) and the Centre for Perioperative Medicine (CPOM) both based
at RDTI. HNAC is the academic arm of the UCLH head and neck cancer unit, aligning with Florence’s clinical
speciality. Florence will also be supported by the NIHR CL-PSRC, with access to the PSRC learning academy
which currently includes five doctoral and three post-doctoral scientists, and which provides mentoring and peer
support from a multidisciplinary team including frontline clinicians, policy makers and academics with a diverse skill
set. The Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Lab (RREAL) also sits within RDTI, and is led by Professor
Vindrola-Padros, Florence’s secondary supervisor. RREAL is uniquely placed to support Florence with the rapid
methodologies she is proposing to undertake. UCL also has a rapidly growing Nutritional Sciences department
within the Division of Medicine which recently opened a pre-registration MSc degree in Dietetics (2021). Florence
will be able to access the expertise, training and equipment available from this de

onsultant in
anaesthetics, perioperative and critical care at UCLH. She has held several prominent leadership positions
including: Director, Royal College of Anaesthetists’ Health Services Research Centre (2016-22); academic training
advisor, National Institute for Academic Anaesthesia (2012-19); training programme director for academic
anaesthesia in London (2012-18); Director NIHR Central London Patient Safety Research Collaboration (2023-28);
theme lead, NIHR UCLH Biomedical Research Centre (2022-27); NHS England’s National Clinical Director for
Critical and Perioperative Care (2020-).
At UCL, she is group lead, Centre Lead and head of the Research Department for Targeted Intervention where my
PhD will be based. She currently has six doctoral students; she has previously supervised six clinicians through to
PhD completion; five more are writing up. She has also been a mentor / advisor to NIHR-funded early/mid-career
researchers in Bristol, Birmingham and London.
She has a longstanding relationship with both Professor Vindrola-Padros and Dr Govender, both of whom she has
supported to academic promotion. She leads research which uses multiple methodologies including quantitative,
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

science. Aligned to this proposal, an approach to enhanced recovery for which she developed the main evidence
has now been adopted by the NHS and supported by NHSE financial incentives.
Formal supervision meetings with all three supervisors will occur monthly. In keeping with UCL guidance I will also
have a thesis committee to ensure that I have consistent external evaluation and support.

Dr Govender is a Consultant Clinical-Academic SLT, an NIHR Clinical-Lecturer and Associate Professor at UCL.
She has over 35 peer-reviewed publications and is a regular invited speaker at national and international
academic conferences. She is Chief-investigator on a NIHR PhaseII multicentre dysphagia trial, chair of the NIHR
Speech Therapy in ENT Group (sub

Dr Govender is currently on the supervisory panel for 3 ICA Doctoral Fellows with the first due to complete next
year. She has supervised 3 PCAFs to completion (all remaining on the research trajectory). In addition to this, she
is supervisor to 4 current awardees on NIHR/HEE infrastructure awards via CRNs or BRCs and one post-doctoral
awardee of the NIHR Knowledge and Skills Enhancement award.
Dr Govender has been formally supervising my clinical academic development since 2021, during my HEE/NIHR
ICA pre-doctoral bridging programme, and UCLH pre-doctoral Chief Nurse Research Fellowship. I have built an
excellent clinical and academic relationship with Dr Govender since my employment at UCLH in 2015. Her SLT
clinical experience will be vital for the toolkit proposed. Dr Govender’s research work straddles the topics of
dysphagia, health behaviour change and health service delivery, all of which will contribute to my own project. She
has experience of both qualitative and quantitative research methods having spent time and received training
within UCL Department of Behavio

Description of support provided
Professor Vindrola-Padros is a Medical Anthropologist and Professorial Research Fellow in the Department of
Targeted Intervention at UCL. She is Director of the Rapid Research Evaluation and Appraisal Lab (RREAL) and
leads a team of 25 researchers interested in applied health research.
Professor Vindrola-Padros’s research themes include cancer, behaviour change, global health and migration with
interests in applied health research, delivery of cancer treatment and development of rapid approaches to
research. She has written extensively on the use of rapid qualitative research and directs the Rapid Research
Evaluation and Appraisal Lab at UCL. She has supervised 10 PhD students to date with 3 PhD completions.
Professor Vindrola-Padros has expertise in ethnographic research in healthcare, rapid qualitative research and
participatory and collaborative res

earch. She is the past director of the Qualitative Health Research Network

a qualitative workstream using interviews and observations, and development of an intervention in cancer.
Supervision will occur monthly, joint with Professor Moonesinghe and Dr Govender either face-to-face or via MS

proposal
I attended workshops through UCLH NIHR BRC from April to December 2022 on what PPI is and how to find/do
PPI. I was awarded a BRC PPI starter-grant (£300) in open competition (August 2022) for this research to form a
PPI group and run an initial group discussion.
I reached out to Barbara Reed, founder of Young Tongues, a support group for people with tongue cancer who
represent the majority affected by my research. Barbara disseminated my PPI advert and advised me to meet
face-to-face due to impact of surgery on speech intelligibility and understanding over a virtual/hybrid model. I
carefully selected five patients from my clinical caseload and Young Tongues, to ensure representation across
ethnicity (two Asian, three White-British), occupation, age (26-74years), gender (three women, two men),
disease/flap characteristics. Ethnicity was particularly important as my research is about eating/drinking post-

r70.
Methodology:
A convergence-coding matrix will be used to integrate findings from WS3 and WS4 to determine areas of
agreement, silence, and dissonance71(Figure 2). Desk-based development of toolkit will then be undertaken using
WS1-4 and convergence findings, MRC logic-modelling and PPI feedback/consultation. Stakeholder consultation
will then be conducted with six staff-representatives involved in decision-making (Consultant and Registrar
Surgeon, Dietitian, Speech-Therapist, ERAS-Nurse, Ward-Nurse) and 2 PPI-members. Questions from the 16-
item NPT toolkit will be used to frame the consultation and generate discussion to refine the toolkit which will be
amended accordingly using a series of iterations.
Analysis and outcome:
The toolkit will be evaluated in post-doctoral work.
Dissemination, Outputs and Impact
Dissemination:
To enter society, health/social care:
 Seven articles (review, survey, cohort study, 2xqualitative studies, toolkit-development, co-produced
paper) published in open-access in peer-reviewed journals targeted to HNC/ERAS readership.
 Presenting locally at UCLH-Support in Cancer Research Forum
 Presenting national/internationally through conferences targe

ted to HNC, nutrition and ERAS clinicians:
o BAHNO: to present the review.
o ERAS-World Congress: to present the survey.
o British Association of Oral/Maxillofacial Surgeons: to present the cohort study.
o BDA-Research Symposium: to present the qualitative studies and build networks for delivering a
future multi-centre study.
 Podcasts for cancer-care e.g., RadChat
 Brief for the NIHR-webpage
 Sharing through professional networks e.g., BDA-HNC Group and social-media e.g., Twitter
To inform/engage patients and the public, an engaging audio-visual animation of findings will be developed and
disseminated through social-media, blogs, support-groups and charities. I will also disseminate at The Swallows
charity patient conference including the impact of PPI in this research.
Outputs and further funding:
This research will produce an evidence-based toolkit to improve post-operative feeding practices and nutritional
intake. A PhD thesis will be deposited in the UCL repository.
Post-doctoral funding will be sought (NIHR DSE/ACAF) to answer the question: ‘Does use of a post-operative
feeding toolkit lead to improved nutritional outcomes for patients with HNC undergoing flap-surgery?’. T

his will
include a multi-centre validation study, feasibility, pilot and definitive trial comparing the toolkit to standard care.
Impact of outputs and barriers:
For clinical stakeholders:
This research could change perioperative HNC feeding management by introducing a novel, evidence-based
toolkit. Barriers to adoption include shifting conservative practices and engaging external centres to support a
multi-centre study. I have considered these issues through:
 Collaboration with international experts experienced in implementing novel ERAS interventions.
 Collaboration with HNC organisations to incorporate findings into national guidance/standards.
 Building HNC networks in my training/dissemination plan and selecting a practice supervisor with well-
established surgical networks to support future multi-centre work.
 Collaboration with our UCLH ERAS-nurse for local implementation.
 Underpinning this work with implementation science theories/frameworks.
For the NHS/patients:
At the end of this PhD, this research should improve nutritional outcomes and safety evidence for transitioning
from tube to oral feeding post-surgery. Local toolkit uptake is anticipated within five-years

and nationwide within
ten, pending an RCT demonstrating effectiveness. Policy impact in anticipated through recommending the toolkit
in National Institute for Health and Care Excellence and ENT-UK clinical guidelines.
Cost-saving is anticipated by avoiding malnutrition and 9% (unplanned) and 72% (planned) readmissions caused
by nutrition. Readmission costs from flap-surgery have been evaluated in the USA ($9,070-$15,916/patient). Most
comparable UK estimates comes from ear/nose/throat surgery (£1052/patient)72. However, average readmission
duration was only 1.3-days (UK) compared to 8-days (USA) which may explain the lower cost. Streamlining oral
feeding commencement should reduce LoS by 3-4days/patient. NHS bed-costs are ~£300-500/day/patient73,
saving £90,000-200,000/year (UCLH) or £5850,000-13,000,000/year (all 65 UK-units).
Project management
My proposal has 5 workstreams over 36-months. My pre-doctoral fellowship and part-time teaching secondment
have provided excellent foundations for managing multiple commitments to time. My GANTT chart demonstrates
how courses and professional practice will be managed alongside. Generous timeframes have been allocated for

WS3: Prospective cohort study [Months 10-29]
Rationale:
Limited empirical research has identified factors associated with timely/adequate nutritional intake after surgery. A
prospective cohort study will identify factors that may be predictive of nutritional adequacy that can be targeted in
the toolkit.
Research question:
What are the factors that contribute to timely and adequate oral intake in patients with HNC transitioning from tube
to oral feeding after undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
An observational single-centre exploratory prospective cohort study will be undertaken at UCLH which is a
centralised London service for four NHS-trusts, covering a wide-geographic area representative of people from
diverse, multiple ethnic backgrounds affected by HNC3,52,53.UCLH offers all discharge pathways for oral/tube
feeding which optimises generalisability, within the limitations of a single-centre design.
Data will be collected from electronic hospital records. All measures are routinely gathered except for the following
patient-recorded measures:
1-week diet diary once oral feeding commences.



estion:
What are the facilitators and barriers for transitioning from tube to adequate oral intake in patients with HNC after
undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
Rapid qualitative research techniques will be utilised to optimise efficiency by running data collection and analysis
in parallel. 1:1 semi-structured interviews will be conducted with patients and healthcare professionals and
unstructured observations of staff practices.
Sample:
Patient interviews:
A subset of patients participating in the cohort study will be invited off-treatment within 6-weeks of surgery.
Patients who have not recommenced oral intake in this timeframe will be excluded. Purposive sampling will be
used to ensure maximal variation in terms of sex, age, ethnicity, disease site/stage, type of flap and feeding tube.
General guidance indicates that 6-12 interviews is usually acceptable with little new information beyond 1560.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a similar study in colorectal cancer, a sample of 10
is anticipated61.

What are the facilitators and barriers for transitioning from tube to adequate oral intake in patients with HNC after
undergoing flap-surgery?
Methodology:
Rapid qualitative research techniques will be utilised to optimise efficiency by running data collection and analysis
in parallel. 1:1 semi-structured interviews will be conducted with patients and healthcare professionals and
unstructured observations of staff practices.
Sample:
Patient interviews:
A subset of patients participating in the cohort study will be invited off-treatment within 6-weeks of surgery.
Patients who have not recommenced oral intake in this timeframe will be excluded. Purposive sampling will be
used to ensure maximal variation in terms of sex, age, ethnicity, disease site/stage, type of flap and feeding tube.
General guidance indicates that 6-12 interviews is usually acceptable with little new information beyond 1560.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a similar study in colorectal cancer, a sample of 10
is anticipated61.
Staff interviews:
Healthcare professionals will be invited to participate using purposive sampling to represent different professional
roles and years

of experience. Sampling will be supported by survey data to capture variation in practices.
Recruitment will be until data saturation is achieved. Based on a study of similar design, a sample of 16-18
healthcare professionals is anticipated38.

What is the problem?
In the UK, 1 in 2 people will have cancer in their lifetime. Disease burden is increasing with more people living
longer with cancer1. The NHS long-term plan identified improving cancer care as a national priority2. Head and
neck cancer (HNC) is the 8th most common cancer in the UK and 4th in men. Incidence has grown by a third in the
last decade3, associated with rises in human-papilloma virus related HNC4. 12,422 people are diagnosed annually
of which 75% require surgery3. Surgery involves removal of important structures used for swallowing which are
then reconstructed using tissue from another part of the body, called a ‘flap’. This is known as ‘flap-tissue
reconstruction’. Following surgery, patients are placed nil-by-mouth (no eating/drinking) to allow surgical healing.
Nutrition is delivered via a feeding tube through the nose (naso-gastric tube) or directly into the stomach
(gastrostomy) until they can recommence oral intake. Current practices are demonstrated in Figure 1 and have
two key problems:
Firstly, recommencing oral intake is likely unnecessarily delayed for most patients. At UCLH, patients wait on
average 11-days5, this could be reduced to 5-da

ys for some patients6. Patients recommence oral intake when the
surgeon is satisfied the site has recovered and the patient can swallow safely, often with input from the speech-
therapist. If not achieved, this could lead to breakdown of the reconstruction, resulting in complications including
wound breakdown, oroctuaneous fistula (passage between oral cavity and skin) or aspiration (passage of food into
the lungs)7. Many surgeons conservatively prolong nil-by-mouth, even if the site has healed as there is limited
evidence to determine when oral intake can be recommenced safely. However, delaying oral intake deconditions
swallowing muscles and could be the difference between eating/drinking again or requiring life-long tube-feeding.
Secondly, patients often experience nutritional deficits when transitioning from tube to oral intake which can lead to
developing malnutrition. This occurs because naso-gastric tubes get removed once oral feeding commences,
irrespective of whether a patient is meeting their nutritional requirements orally8, as naso-gastric tubes can
prevent/delay discharge9. Some patients continue with tube-feeding at home but are generally those needing to
remain nil-b

y-mouth, rather than those who can eat/drink but aren’t meeting their nutritional requirements.
This proposal aims to improve this pathway by filling evidence-gaps in post-operative feeding practices, to avoid
delayed and inadequate intake when transitioning from tube to oral feeding. This aligns with the 2019 James-Lind
Alliance Nutritional Screening and Malnutrition research priority ‘can nutritional-interventions lead to health
improvements’10.
Why is this research important?
This research falls under enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) initiatives. It should optimise post-operative
nutrition11with
feeding practices and patient, service, and economic benefits:
Patients benefits:
 Improved nutritional status by avoiding post-operative nutritional deficit; a modifiable risk factor for
malnutrition which is negatively associated with muscle-mass, immune response, wound healing, fatigue,
complications, morbidity/mortality, length-of-stay (LoS) and return-to-work12.
 Improved swallowing rehabilitation by reducing inactivity time which deconditions swallow
musculature13.This could be the difference between rehabilitating swallow or requiring life-long tube-
feeding8,14.
 Improve

d quality-of-life due to improved independence with eating/drinking15.

recovery16.
resonates with patients, family and carers, associating eating with

studies5,7,9,30,31and one pilot randomised controlled trial (RCT)32have been conducted in HNC flap-surgery with
another RCT registered (NCT04787939). These are limited by inconsistent definitions of early oral feeding (which
a registered scoping review aims to determine33) and selection bias6. Firstly, only patients without a tracheostomy
were randomised to early oral feeding. Tracheostomy-use varies between centres. In centres that treat early-stage
disease, tracheostomy-use is 13%9. In centres that treat more advanced-stage disease, tracheostomy-use is
